rows.v: The US Dollar ($), the world's primary reserve currency and the most widely used in international trade and finance.
The dollar's dominance makes it the default currency for global commodities like oil and for central bank reserves worldwide.
Its name traces to the 'thaler,' a silver coin from a Bohemian valley ('Tal'); the dollar sign's origin is debated, possibly from the Spanish peso.
The Euro (€), the shared currency of much of the European Union, and the second-most-held reserve currency in the world.
Introduced for accounting in 1999 and as cash in 2002, the euro unified the currencies of numerous European nations into one.
The currency symbol was inspired by the Greek letter epsilon and the first letter of 'Europe,' with two parallel lines signifying stability.
The Japanese Yen (¥), one of the world's most traded currencies and a major reserve asset.
The yen is often treated by investors as a 'safe haven,' strengthening in times of global financial uncertainty.
The name derives from the Japanese word for a round object; the currency was adopted in 1871 as Japan modernized its economy.
The British Pound Sterling (£), the world's oldest currency still in use, and a major reserve currency.
Sterling has been in continuous use for over 1,200 years, making it the oldest currency still circulating today.
The name reflects that a pound originally equaled a pound weight of sterling silver; the '£' symbol comes from the Latin 'libra,' meaning scales or weight.
The Swiss Franc (CHF), prized for its stability and long associated with safety and discretion.
Like the yen, the franc is considered a safe-haven currency, sought by investors during global turmoil.
Its abbreviation comes from 'Confoederatio Helvetica,' the Latin name for Switzerland, which avoids favoring any of the nation's official languages.
The Chinese Yuan or Renminbi (¥), the official currency of the world's second-largest economy and a growing reserve asset.
'Renminbi' means 'people's currency' and is the official name, while 'yuan' refers to a single unit of it.
China has actively promoted the yuan's international use, and it was added to the IMF's basket of major reserve currencies in 2016.
The Canadian Dollar (C$), often nicknamed the 'loonie' after the bird on its one-dollar coin.
The loonie's value is closely tied to commodity prices, especially oil, given Canada's resource-rich economy.
The nickname comes from the common loon depicted on the gold-colored coin; the two-dollar coin is affectionately called the 'toonie.'
The Mexican Peso ($), one of the most traded currencies in Latin America and among emerging markets.
The peso is among the most liquid and widely traded of all emerging-market currencies.
The very '$' sign now used for the dollar likely derives from the Spanish peso, which circulated widely, making the peso an ancestor of modern dollar notation.
The Brazilian Real (R$), the currency of South America's largest economy.
The real was introduced in 1994 as part of a successful plan to tame Brazil's catastrophic hyperinflation.
Its name means 'royal' and harks back to an old colonial-era Portuguese currency, revived for the modern stabilization effort.
The Argentine Peso ($), a currency long shaped by recurring inflation and economic volatility.
Argentina's peso has endured repeated bouts of severe inflation, making currency stability a persistent national challenge.
The country has changed and redenominated its currency several times over the decades in response to economic crises.
The Chilean Peso ($), the currency of one of South America's most stable economies.
Chile's peso reflects an economy heavily influenced by copper, of which the country is the world's largest producer.
Like many regional currencies, it descends from the Spanish colonial peso, a lineage shared across much of Latin America.
The Swedish Krona (kr), the currency of Sweden, which has chosen not to adopt the euro.
Despite being an EU member, Sweden retains its own krona after a 2003 referendum rejected joining the euro.
'Krona' means 'crown,' a name shared by several Nordic currencies reflecting their historic monarchies.
The Norwegian Krone (kr), a currency bolstered by the nation's vast oil wealth.
Norway's krone is supported by one of the world's largest sovereign wealth funds, built from North Sea oil revenues.
Like its Nordic neighbors, the name means 'crown'; Norway, not an EU member, has kept its independent currency throughout.
The Polish Zloty (zł), the currency of one of Central Europe's largest economies.
Poland, an EU member, continues to use the zloty rather than adopting the euro.
The name means 'golden,' originally referring to medieval gold coins that circulated in the region centuries ago.
The Turkish Lira (₺), a currency that has faced significant inflation and volatility in recent years.
The lira has experienced sharp depreciation amid economic turbulence, a major concern for Turkey's economy.
The name derives, like the Italian lira, from the Latin 'libra' (a pound weight); a modern symbol was introduced in 2012.
The Russian Ruble (₽), one of the oldest currencies in Europe, dating back centuries.
The ruble is among the oldest national currencies still in use, with roots stretching back to the medieval era.
The name may come from a verb meaning 'to chop,' as early rubles were reportedly cut from larger pieces of silver.
The Icelandic Krona (kr), the currency of one of the world's smallest independent currency areas.
Iceland's tiny, isolated currency was at the center of the nation's dramatic 2008 banking collapse.
Another Nordic 'crown' currency, it serves a population smaller than many cities, making it one of the world's most unusual sovereign currencies.
The Indian Rupee (₹), the currency of the world's most populous nation.
The rupee serves one of the world's largest and fastest-growing economies and populations.
The name comes from a Sanskrit word for wrought silver; a distinctive modern symbol, blending Latin and Devanagari script, was adopted in 2010.
The South Korean Won (₩), the currency of a major global technology and manufacturing economy.
The won underpins one of Asia's most advanced economies, home to global electronics and automotive giants.
The name shares a root with the Japanese yen and Chinese yuan, all derived from a word meaning 'round object' or coin.
The Singapore Dollar (S$), the currency of one of the world's leading financial centers.
The Singapore dollar reflects the city-state's status as a global hub for trade, banking, and finance.
It maintains an unusual currency interchangeability agreement with Brunei, where each nation accepts the other's notes at par.
The Thai Baht (฿), the currency of Thailand and a significant Southeast Asian currency.
The baht was at the epicenter of the 1997 Asian financial crisis, which began when Thailand floated its currency.
The name once referred to a traditional unit of weight for silver and gold, still used for measuring gold in Thailand today.
The Vietnamese Dong (₫), notable for having one of the highest denominations in everyday use.
Years of past inflation mean even modest purchases in Vietnam can involve tens of thousands of dong.
The name derives from a term for 'copper,' the metal of historic coinage; large-denomination banknotes are a feature of daily life.
The Indonesian Rupiah (Rp), the currency of Southeast Asia's largest economy.
Like the dong, the rupiah trades in very large numbers, with common notes in the tens of thousands.
Its name, like India's rupee, descends from the Sanskrit word for silver, reflecting shared historical roots across South and Southeast Asia.
The South African Rand (R), the most traded currency on the African continent.
The rand is widely used across southern Africa and serves as a benchmark for the region's economies.
It is named after the Witwatersrand, the gold-rich ridge near Johannesburg whose mines built much of the nation's wealth.
The Nigerian Naira (₦), the currency of Africa's most populous nation and one of its largest economies.
The naira serves a vast and youthful population in a major oil-producing economy.
Introduced in 1973 to replace the Nigerian pound, its name is a contraction of 'Nigeria' itself.
The Egyptian Pound (£ or E£), one of the oldest currencies in the Arab world.
The Egyptian pound reflects a long monetary history in a nation central to the region's economy.
Subdivided into 100 piastres, it carries a name and structure inherited from the era when many regional currencies followed a pound standard.
The UAE Dirham (د.إ), the currency of the United Arab Emirates, a major Gulf financial center.
The dirham is pegged to the US dollar, providing stability for the UAE's trade- and tourism-driven economy.
The name 'dirham' descends from the ancient Greek 'drachma,' a coin name that spread across the Middle East over many centuries.
The Saudi Riyal (﷼), the currency of the world's largest oil exporter.
The riyal is pegged to the US dollar, reflecting the central role of dollar-priced oil in Saudi Arabia's economy.
The name comes from the 'real,' the Spanish silver coin that once circulated globally, linking Gulf currency to the age of world trade.
The Israeli New Shekel (₪), a currency whose name is among the most ancient in recorded history.
The shekel ranks among the more stable and widely traded currencies in the Middle East.
'Shekel' was a unit of weight and currency in the ancient Near East thousands of years ago, revived for the modern state's money.
The Kenyan Shilling (KSh), one of the most prominent currencies in East Africa.
The shilling is a key trading currency across the East African region.
It retains the name 'shilling' from the British colonial era, a unit once common throughout Britain's former territories worldwide.
